skill.name: geniml
skill.description: Guides genomic interval machine learning workflows with geniml, including Region2Vec, BEDspace, scEmbed, consensus peak universes, tokenization, evaluation, and BED utilities.
skill.summary: Run genomic interval machine learning workflows with geniml.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows they are primarily markdown examples for geniml, Python, R, and shell workflows. No prompt injection, credential access, destructive command, or malicious exfiltration intent was found. Publication is acceptable with a warning because users may install packages, run local commands, download remote models or data, and write cache files.

medium_findings.title: User-Directed Local Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill contains shell commands for installing geniml and running genomic analysis tools. These are markdown examples, not autonomous execution, but users who follow them will execute local commands and install software.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly present in fenced examples and are relevant to the documented tool. I found no evidence that the skill runs them automatically or hides command injection behavior.

medium_findings.title: Remote Package, Model, and Data Access
medium_findings.description: The documentation points users to install code from GitHub, use StarSpace, load Hugging Face models, and fetch BED data through BBClient. These are legitimate workflows, but they create supply-chain and data provenance risks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network references are explicit and semantically tied to package, model, and data retrieval. They are not evidence of exfiltration, but they do require external trust decisions.

medium_findings.title: Local Cache Writes Under Home Directory
medium_findings.description: The BBClient examples configure a cache folder under the user home directory. This appears to be normal caching, but marketplace users should know the workflow writes local files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cache path is directly shown in Python and R examples. The context supports benign caching rather than hidden persistence.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker Labels Were False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts map to genomics or machine learning prose, parameters, and examples. No cryptographic API use, system inventory collection, network scanning, or prompt injection text was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review of the cited lines found scientific descriptions, command examples, and parameter text. No code or instruction at those locations performs weak cryptography or reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Direct Installation and CLI Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes install and CLI examples that users may copy into a shell. Review packages and inputs before running them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and easy for users to run, but they are not automatically executed by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Resource Loading Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents remote installs, model loading, and data fetching. These operations should use trusted sources and pinned versions where possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows legitimate remote dependency and data workflows, not covert networking or credential transfer.

content.value_statement: Genomic interval machine learning requires many tools and careful workflow choices. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan geniml pipelines for BED files, embeddings, scATAC-seq, and consensus peaks.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Region2Vec, BEDspace, scEmbed, consensus peak building, and geniml utilities.
Provides stepwise workflows for tokenizing BED files and training genomic region embeddings.
Shows CLI and Python examples for universe building, BEDspace search, and scATAC-seq embedding workflows.
Describes evaluation metrics and validation checks for trained embeddings and universe quality.
Documents utility patterns for caching BED files, randomizing regions, and building search indexes.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not include executable helper scripts.
Users must install geniml, StarSpace, and scientific dependencies separately.
It does not validate biological correctness or dataset quality by itself.
Remote data, model, and package sources require independent trust review.

use_cases.title: Train Region Embeddings
use_cases.description: Plan a Region2Vec workflow for BED files, including tokenization, training parameters, and embedding evaluation.

use_cases.title: Analyze Single-Cell ATAC-seq
use_cases.description: Use scEmbed guidance to prepare AnnData, tokenize cells, train embeddings, and integrate results with scanpy.

use_cases.title: Build Consensus Peak Universes
use_cases.description: Compare CC, CCF, ML, and HMM universe methods before creating standardized reference peak sets.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Geniml Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: I have BED files and want to use geniml. Ask me the minimum questions needed, then recommend the right workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Region2Vec Training
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Region2Vec training plan for my BED collection. Include universe preparation, tokenization, parameters, and validation checks.

prompt_templates.title: Design a scEmbed Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a scEmbed workflow for an AnnData scATAC-seq dataset. Include preprocessing, training, clustering, and quality control.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Universe Methods
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare CC, CCF, ML, and HMM universe building for my dataset. Recommend parameters and explain tradeoffs for publication-quality analysis.

output_examples.input: I need embeddings for 500 ATAC-seq BED files.
output: Recommended workflow: build or select a universe, run hard tokenization, train Region2Vec, then evaluate clustering metrics.
Key checks: genome assembly consistency, tokenization coverage above 80 percent, and stable embedding quality across parameter sweeps.

output_examples.input: I want to cluster single-cell ATAC-seq cells with geniml.
output: Recommended workflow: prepare AnnData peak coordinates, pre-tokenize cells, train scEmbed, store embeddings, and run scanpy neighbors and UMAP.
Validate clusters with known markers, reference labels, and multiple embedding dimensions.

output_examples.input: Which consensus peak method should I use for noisy datasets?
output: Start with coverage cutoff for a baseline, then use CCF when peak boundaries are uncertain.
Use ML or HMM only when the extra compute cost is justified by complex peak structure or publication requirements.

best_practices: Confirm genome assembly, coordinate conventions, and universe compatibility before tokenization.
Record package versions, parameters, random seeds, and source datasets for reproducible results.
Validate outputs with coverage metrics, clustering metrics, and biological controls before interpretation.
anti_patterns: Do not train embeddings before checking tokenization coverage and input BED quality.
Do not mix genome assemblies or universe files across one analysis workflow.
Do not install unpinned remote packages or models in sensitive environments without review.

faq.question: What is geniml used for?
faq.answer: Geniml supports machine learning workflows for genomic interval data, especially BED files and chromatin accessibility datasets.

faq.question: Does this skill run geniml automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and workflow guidance. Users decide which commands or Python examples to run.

faq.question: Which tools does the skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers Region2Vec, BEDspace, scEmbed, consensus peak universe building, tokenization, evaluation, BEDshift, and BBClient.

faq.question: Can it help with single-cell ATAC-seq?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains scEmbed workflows for AnnData preparation, cell tokenization, embedding training, clustering, and scanpy integration.

faq.question: What inputs do I need?
faq.answer: Typical inputs include BED files, genome assembly information, a universe BED file, metadata tables, or an AnnData object.

faq.question: What security precautions should users take?
faq.answer: Review remote packages, models, and datasets before use. Run commands in controlled environments and avoid sensitive data leakage.
